content:
===BEGIN===
Blockchain often gets a bad rap for being overhyped or confusing, especially when people hear it doesn’t rely on a central bank or authority. Here’s the straightforward truth: blockchain is essentially a digital ledger shared across many computers worldwide. Instead of one central bank keeping track of transactions, this ledger is stored on thousands of computers, called nodes, all working together.

When someone makes a transaction, it’s grouped with others into a “block.” These blocks are then verified by multiple nodes using complex math, ensuring everyone agrees the transaction is valid. Once confirmed, the block is added to a chain of previous blocks—hence, “blockchain.” Because this ledger is distributed, no single entity controls it or can tamper with it without others noticing.

So, it’s not about hype or magic. It’s a transparent, decentralized way to record transactions securely without needing a central bank to oversee everything.
